input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat: LLM-judge rubric layer for the eval harness" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Direction **#3b**: the opt-in **LLM-as-judge** dimension layered on the deterministic eval harness (#19). In the **🧪 Eval** view, a **⚖️ Run LLM judge** button re-fetches with `?judge=true` and the panel adds:

- **Average rubric scores** (1–5) — faithfulness, actionability, clarity
- **Per-session ratings** with a one-line justification

The deterministic checks remain the always-on, fast backbone; the judge is the subjective layer you opt into. Together they give both objective gates and a rubric to A/B model/prompt changes against.

## How

- **`AnalysisJudgeService`** (`@AiService`): rates one analysis against a bounded *"what happened"* digest of its transcript (user asks, tool calls, errors — capped 6000 chars), sharing the `ChatModel` with `AnalyzerService`.
- **`EvalService.forFlavor(flavor, judge)`**: deterministic pass unchanged. When the judge is requested **and** `AiConfig.isConfigured()`, it rates a **capped sample** (`JUDGE_MAX_SESSIONS=12`) of analyzed sessions **in parallel** on the IO pool (`Semaphore(6)` — the same pattern `AnalysisController` uses), **clamps** scores to `[1,5]`, and averages them.
- **Graceful degradation** (a `JudgeSummary.notRun(note)`, never an error) when: judge not requested / nothing analyzed / AI unconfigured / every judge call throws.
- Records `JudgeScore` (LLM output), `JudgedCase`, `JudgeSummary`; `EvalReport` gains a `judge` field. `GET /api/eval?flavor=&judge=`.
- Frontend `eval.js`: rubric cards + per-case rows when judged, else the button + note.

## Tests

- `EvalServiceTest` — clamping out-of-range scores, cap at `JUDGE_MAX_SESSIONS`, not-configured skip, all-calls-fail degrade, not-requested, nothing-to-judge (a `FORBIDDEN_JUDGE` fake asserts the judge is never called on the no-judge paths)
- `EvalControllerTest` — judge opt-in default + graceful degrade without a key
- `eval.test.js` — Run-judge button/note, rubric render, click re-fetches with `judge=true`, XSS escaping
- `e2e/eval.spec.js` — the judge affordance renders

Verification: `./gradlew build` (Spotless + JUnit) ✅ · 65 frontend ✅ · 25 e2e ✅

## Review

A code-review pass — focused on the thread-pool starvation question (eval runs on an IO thread, submits more IO tasks, blocks on them) — found **no MUST-FIX**. It confirmed this is byte-for-byte `AnalysisController`'s existing pattern on Micronaut's unbounded cached IO pool, so it cannot deadlock; the clamp/average math, graceful degradation, and null-safety all check out.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. **#3b — the LLM-judge rubric layer — is merged to `main`** ([PR #20](https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/pull/20), `181dde9`). Branch pruned, main synced.

## What you can do now
In the **🧪 Eval** view, click **⚖️ Run LLM judge**. It rates a capped sample of your analyzed sessions and adds:
- **Average rubric** (1–5) — **faithfulness**, **actionability**, **clarity**
- **Per-session ratings** with a one-line justification

The deterministic checks stay the always-on, free backbone; the judge is the opt-in subjective layer. Both are labeled with the active model, so the full A/B loop is now: switch model/prompt → *Recompute Analysis* → *Eval* (+ optional judge) → compare deterministic score **and** rubric.

It's safe and cheap by default: the judge only fires when you ask, caps at 12 sessions, runs them in parallel, and degrades to a note (never an error) if AI isn't configured or the model doesn't respond.

## The full arc is complete
**#1 Insights** (observe) → **#2 Miner** (act: skills + AGENTS.md rules) → **#3 Eval** (measure: deterministic) → **#3b Judge** (measure: LLM rubric). Four PRs (#17–#20), each shipped through the same disciplined workflow: tests-first → build + 65 frontend + 25 E2E green → code review (no MUST-FIX across all four) → CI green → squash-merge → cleanup.

The remaining teed-up follow-ups, in rough priority:
1. **Normalize Codex/Claude error text** (carried since #17) so their "common errors" in Insights/Mine aren't over-granular.
2. **Persist eval runs** so scoreboards + rubric can be diffed over time (a true A/B history) rather than compared by eye.
3. **Extract the shared brain-path logic** (minor duplication first flagged in #17).

Want me to pick one of these up, or pause here?
Yes lets do 1 then we can come back and do 2 and 3

On to follow-up **#1: normalize Codex/Claude error text** so their "Most Common Errors" cluster instead of fragmenting. The grouping key lives in `FleetInsights.errorKey`, which is tuned for Antigravity's format — raw Codex/Claude tool-error text (paths, line numbers, PIDs, hex) makes every error its own bucket. Let me first see how those adapters shape error steps.

Now I understand the shapes: both Codex and Claude emit error steps as `FUNCTION_OUTPUT` with `status=ERROR` and the raw tool output/stderr in `content` (paths, line numbers, PIDs, exit codes) — so `errorKey`'s "first line, truncated" fragments them. The fix: a pure `ErrorNormalizer` that picks the salient error line and canonicalizes volatile tokens (paths, numbers, hex, UUIDs, quoted values), which `FleetInsights.errorKey` delegates to. Let me branch and build it tests-first.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix: normalize error text so common errors cluster

The "Most Common Errors" tally keyed on the first line of an error truncated
to 100 chars — tuned for Antigravity's terse step errors. Raw Codex/Claude
tool errors are full stderr/stack-trace dumps whose only difference between two
runs of the same failure is volatile detail (paths, line numbers, PIDs, ports,
hex, UUIDs, the specific quoted value), so each got its own bucket.

Add a pure ErrorNormalizer that picks the most salient line of the blob
(preferring a strong error signature over noise) and canonicalizes volatile
tokens to placeholders — e.g. two `ENOENT ... open '/a/x.txt'` /
`... open '/b/y.txt'` failures both normalize to `... open '<v>'` and count as
one. Alphanumeric codes (e.g. TS2304) are preserved; the scan is length-bounded.
FleetInsights.errorKey now delegates to it.

Tests: ErrorNormalizerTest (quoted values, paths, numbers, hex/uuid, salient-line
preference, prefix/metadata stripping, length cap, blank), plus a FleetInsights
test that two Codex/Claude-style errors collapse into one bucket.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git push -u origin fix/normalize-error-grouping 2>&1 | tail -3
input.description: Commit and push the normalization branch

result.output:
To https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer.git

input.command:
gh pr create --title "fix: normalize error text so common errors cluster" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Follow-up #1 (carried since #17): the Insights **Most Common Errors** tally fragmented for Codex/Claude Code sources. Its grouping key took the first line of an error, truncated to 100 chars — fine for Antigravity's terse step errors, but raw Codex/Claude tool errors are full stderr/stack-trace dumps whose only difference between two runs of the *same* failure is volatile detail (absolute paths, line numbers, PIDs, ports, hex addresses, UUIDs, the specific quoted value). So each near-identical failure got its own bucket.

## How

New pure, source-agnostic **`ErrorNormalizer`**:
1. **Picks the salient line** of the blob — prefers a strong error signature (`error`/`exception`/`cannot`/`no such file`/`permission denied`/`traceback`/`panic`/`npm err`/…) over a noisy first line or a bare exit-code line; skips Antigravity metadata (`Created At:` …) and strips its `Encountered error in step execution:` prefix.
2. **Canonicalizes volatile tokens** → placeholders: `0x…`→`<hex>`, UUID→`<id>`, quoted values→`'<v>'`, slash-paths→`<path>`, standalone integers→`<n>`. Alphanumeric codes (e.g. `TS2304`) are preserved; the scan is length-bounded to keep the regex cheap on huge single-line outputs.

`FleetInsights.errorKey` now delegates to it (the `RUN_COMMAND` special-case is unchanged). Example: `ENOENT ... open '/Users/alice/a.txt'` and `... open '/home/bob/b.txt'` both normalize to `Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open '<v>'` and count as **one** error with count 2.

## Tests

- `ErrorNormalizerTest` (9) — quoted-value/path/number/hex/uuid collapsing, TS-code preservation, salient-line preference, weak-line fallback, prefix+metadata stripping, length cap, blank/null → `Unknown error`
- `FleetInsightsTest` — two Codex/Claude-style (`content`-based) errors collapse into a single bucket with count 2

Backend-only change (no frontend/API shape change). Verification: `./gradlew build` (Spotless + JUnit) ✅ · 65 frontend ✅ · 25 e2e ✅ (existing `NullPointerException at X` grouping is unaffected — no volatile tokens).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. **Follow-up #1 — error-text normalization — is merged to `main`** ([PR #21](https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/pull/21), `4b5f5fc`). Branch pruned, main synced.

## What changed
Insights' **Most Common Errors** now clusters near-identical Codex/Claude failures instead of fragmenting. The new pure `ErrorNormalizer` picks the salient error line (preferring a real error signature over noise) and canonicalizes the volatile bits — paths, line numbers, PIDs/ports, hex, UUIDs, quoted values — to placeholders. So two runs of the same failure that differed only by a file path or a number now collapse into one bucket with the right count. Alphanumeric codes like `TS2304` are preserved, and the scan is length-bounded so a huge single-line stderr blob can't make the regex expensive.

Backend-only, no API/UI shape change. Verified: `./gradlew build` (Spotless + JUnit, +10 tests) · 65 frontend · 25 E2E — all green, and existing Antigravity grouping is untouched (no volatile tokens to strip).

## Where we are
Merged this session: **#17 Insights → #18 Miner → #19 Eval → #20 Judge → #21 error normalization** — five PRs, each through the full tests-first → review → CI-green → squash-merge workflow.

Remaining from the follow-up list, which you said we'd come back to:
2. **Persist eval runs** so scoreboards + rubric can be diffed over time (a real A/B history) rather than compared by eye.
3. **Extract the shared Antigravity brain-path logic** (minor duplication across `AnalysisController` / `SessionCollector`).

Want to take **#2 (persist eval runs)** next, or **#3 (the brain-path cleanup)** first since it's the quicker one?
